did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a deep learning model based on the CLIP ViT-B/16 architecture to classify basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from seborrheic keratosis (SK) using dermoscopic images. The model's predictions were used to assist dermatologists, and its performance was evaluated using AUC, NRI, and IDI metrics.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and describes a prospective study involving 707 internal patients and 5,572 external patients from the ISIC dataset. It details original methods (CLIP ViT-B/16 model training, preprocessing, statistical analysis with NRI/IDI), results (AUC metrics, sensitivity/specificity values), and conclusions based on new experimental data, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep learning in assisting dermatologists in classifying basal cell carcinoma from seborrheic keratosis
brief_summary: This prospective study evaluated the effectiveness of a deep learning model based on the CLIP ViT-B/16 architecture in assisting dermatologists to differentiate basal cell carcinoma from seborrheic keratosis. The model was trained and tested on an external dataset of 5,572 patients from the ISIC archive and validated on an internal cohort of 707 histopathologically confirmed patients. Performance was assessed using area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), net reclassification index (NRI), and integrated discrimination improvement (IDI). The deep learning model achieved AUCs of 0.76, 0.79, and 0.71 in the training, test, and validation cohorts, respectively. When assisting dermatologists, the model significantly improved diagnostic accuracy, with NRI and IDI analyses showing statistically significant improvements in reclassification performance for both experienced and less experienced dermatologists. The study concludes that deep learning models have significant potential to aid dermatologists in classifying BCC from SK, enhancing diagnostic accuracy and interpretability through attention mechanisms.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant skin cancer; one of the two primary classes in the binary classification task. Included in internal dataset (707 patients) and external ISIC dataset (5,572 patients). Histopathological features mentioned: atypical keratinocytes, parakeratosis, irregular stratum corneum, light-colored damaged nodular hyperplasia.

Benign lesion; one of the two primary classes in the binary classification task. Included in internal dataset (707 patients) and external ISIC dataset (5,572 patients). Histopathological features mentioned: acanthosis, hyperkeratosis, horn cysts, black papillomatous hyperplasia.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and seborrheic keratosis (SK)
all_task_types.details: The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (BCC vs. SK) using dermoscopic images. Outputs are converted into a probability distribution via a softmax layer for class prediction. Performance was evaluated using AUC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV.

datasets_used.name: Internal Dataset (Jinshan Hospital)
datasets_used.description: A prospective internal cohort of patients with histopathologically confirmed BCC or SK, recruited between November 1, 2023, and November 1, 2024, from Jinshan Hospital of Fudan University. Images were captured via dermoscopy and smart devices.

use.validation: Used as the validation cohort to evaluate the model's performance and verify the effectiveness of the deep learning model in assisting dermatologists.
use.other: Used for comparative analysis with dermatologists (Dermatologist 1 and Dermatologist 2) to assess diagnostic accuracy improvements using NRI and IDI metrics.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration dataset accessed from https://www.isic-archive.com. Images with visible artifacts or interference from nearby structures were excluded. It included images from dermoscopy and other devices.

use.training: Used as the external dataset, split into training and test cohorts in a 9:1 ratio. The training cohort (5038 patients) was used to train the CLIP ViT-B/16 model.
use.testing: The test cohort (558 patients) was used to test the model's performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify the improvement in reclassification performance and diagnostic accuracy when dermatologists used the deep learning model's predictions compared to their initial assessments.
To quantify the improvement in discrimination performance when dermatologists used the deep learning model's predictions compared to their initial assessments.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the model's and dermatologists' discrimination ability to differentiate between basal cell carcinoma and seborrheic keratosis.
To provide uncertainty estimates for the AUC values of the model and dermatologists.
To assess the normality of the data.
To evaluate the homogeneity of variance.
To compare groups for continuous variables when data met assumptions of normality and homoscedasticity.
To compare groups for continuous variables when assumptions of normality and homoscedasticity were not satisfied.

statistical_methods_used.method: Chi-squared test or Fisher’s exact test
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze categorical variables, selected based on expected cell counts.
To determine statistical significance in all analyses.

hypothesis_testing.test: Net reclassification index (NRI) and Integrated discrimination improvement (IDI) tests, along with independent-samples t-tests, Mann-Whitney U test, Chi-squared test, and Fisher’s exact test.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no improvement in reclassification performance (NRI = 0) or discrimination performance (IDI = 0) when dermatologists use the deep learning model's predictions compared to their initial assessments. For other comparisons, the null hypothesis is that there is no difference between groups (e.g., no difference in means for continuous variables or no association for categorical variables).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant improvement in reclassification performance (NRI > 0) or discrimination performance (IDI > 0) when dermatologists use the deep learning model's predictions compared to their initial assessments. For other comparisons, the alternative hypothesis is that there is a difference between groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined using a p-value threshold of less than 0.05. The NRI and IDI analyses revealed statistically significant improvements in diagnostic accuracy and reclassification for both dermatologists when assisted by the deep learning model (e.g., Dermatologist 1 NRI p=0.006, IDI p<0.001; Dermatologist 2 NRI p=0.049, IDI p<0.001).

ISIC_Public_Dataset_Training_Cohort.description: External dataset, 5038 patients (1916 females, 3113 males, 9 missing), avg age 65 ± 14.6 years. Split into training (5014 patients) and test (558 patients) cohorts in 9:1 ratio.
ISIC_Public_Dataset_Training_Cohort.notes: Moderate discrimination ability. Model relied on color features (SK: light brown/brown; BCC: light red/dark red) and surface features (SK: black papillomatous hyperplasia; BCC: light-colored damaged nodular hyperplasia).

ISIC_Public_Dataset_Test_Cohort.description: External dataset test cohort, 558 patients (200 females, 334 males), avg age 65 ± 15.6 years. SK group: 135 females, 244 males (avg age 66 ± 15.3). BCC group: 65 females, 90 males (avg age 62 ± 15.9).
ISIC_Public_Dataset_Test_Cohort.notes: Model demonstrated robustness when applied to new data.

Internal_Dataset_Validation_Cohort.description: Prospective internal cohort, 707 patients (389 females, 318 males), avg age 56 ± 13.6 years. SK group: 610 patients (328 females, 280 males, avg age 56 ± 13.4). BCC group: 97 patients (61 females, 38 males, avg age 60 ± 14.2).
Internal_Dataset_Validation_Cohort.notes: Dermatologist 1 showed 18% improvement in reclassification. Dermatologist 2 showed 11% improvement. NRI and IDI analyses revealed statistically significant improvements for both dermatologists when assisted by the model. Model misclassifications attributed to color blending, blurry images, interference from similar colors, and small sample-specific features.

architecture_details.encoder_type: Transformer encoder with self-attention mechanisms and fully connected feed-forward networks
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Images divided into fixed-size patches, mapped to low-dimensional space via learnable linear projection, position encodings added, processed by Transformer encoder, feature pooling to fixed-length vector, projected via fully connected layer
architecture_details.fine_tuning_method: CoOp (Context Optimization), a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique freezing backbone parameters and introducing learnable text prompt parameters at the input of the text encoder
architecture_details.text_encoder_integration: Class names integrated into prompts with learnable parameters, fed into text encoder to generate feature vectors

classification_or_output_head.type: Linear classifier attached to CLIP image encoder
classification_or_output_head.output_processing: Softmax layer to convert outputs into probability distribution

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to a standard size compatible with the input layer of the deep learning model
Conversion of images into tensor format
Data augmentation techniques including random cropping, rotation, flipping, and color adjustments
Normalization involving subtracting the mean value of the entire dataset and dividing by the standard deviation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to provide image information from varying locations.
Used to help the model recognize objects from different orientations.
Used to help the model recognize objects from different orientations.
Used to increase the model’s robustness to changes in lighting and color variations.

conclusions: The CLIP-based deep learning model demonstrated significant potential in aiding dermatologists in classifying basal cell carcinoma (BCC) from seborrheic keratosis (SK).
The deep learning model achieved AUCs of 0.76, 0.79, and 0.71 in the training, test, and validation cohorts, respectively.

Assistance from the deep learning model significantly improved the diagnostic accuracy of both experienced (15 years) and less experienced (3 years) dermatologists, with AUCs increasing from 0.75 to 0.82 and 0.79 to 0.82, respectively.

Net reclassification index (NRI) and integrated discrimination improvement (IDI) analyses revealed statistically significant improvements in reclassification performance for both dermatologists when assisted by the model.
Attention mechanisms like Grad-CAM provided insights into the model’s decision-making process, enhancing the interpretability of its predictions.

The model primarily relied on color features (e.g., light brown/brown for SK vs. light red/dark red for BCC) and surface features (e.g., black papillomatous hyperplasia for SK vs. light-colored damaged nodular hyperplasia for BCC) for classification.

The model's performance was limited by factors such as color blending, blurry images, interference from similar colors, and small sample-specific features, indicating a need for future training iterations to address these issues.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a skewed gender distribution within the dataset, which may limit the generalizability of findings across more diverse populations.

The model's performance was limited by factors such as color blending, blurry images, interference from similar colors, and small sample-specific features, indicating a need for future training iterations.

Variations in image acquisition protocols, device resolutions, and population demographics across institutions may influence performance; the study suggests incorporating detailed information on imaging devices in future work.
The internal validation cohort consisted of only 707 patients, which is a relatively small sample size for clinical validation.
The model relied heavily on color features (e.g., light brown/brown for SK vs. light red/dark red for BCC) and surface features, which can be confounded by similar colors or image quality issues.
The study did not report external validation across multiple independent institutions or diverse geographic regions, relying instead on a single internal hospital cohort and the ISIC public dataset.

The deep learning model achieved moderate discrimination ability (AUC 0.76-0.79) in the external ISIC dataset, which is lower than the performance observed in assisting dermatologists in the internal cohort.
The study did not address potential data leakage risks between the training/test splits of the ISIC dataset and the internal validation cohort, although they are distinct sources.
